children.name: Otis
children.description: The great bustard — a monotypic genus containing the heaviest flying bird on Earth. Male Great Bustards can weigh up to 21 kg, yet they fly powerfully with deep, slow wingbeats. Once widespread across European grasslands, they have declined dramatically due to agricultural intensification.

children.name: Otis tarda
children.description: The heaviest flying bird on Earth, with males reaching 21 kg. Brown above, white below, with a grey head and neck. In display, the male inflates his throat sac, raises his tail, and transforms into a white feathery ball visible from kilometres away. Once widespread across Europe, now reduced to fragmented populations in Spain, Portugal, and Central Asia. Occasionally recorded in Sweden.

children.name: Chlamydotis
children.description: The houbara bustards — medium-sized bustards of arid regions, known for their spectacular courtship displays in which males raise a white ruff of neck feathers and run in circles. They are highly prized by falconers in the Middle East, leading to intensive conservation breeding programmes.

children.name: Chlamydotis undulata
children.description: Found in North Africa and the Canary Islands. Sandy-brown above, white below, with a distinctive black-and-white neck ruff. The male's courtship display involves raising the ruff, drooping the wings, and striding in circles. Classified as Vulnerable due to hunting and habitat loss.

children.name: Chlamydotis macqueenii
children.description: Formerly considered conspecific with the African Houbara. Ranges from the Middle East through Central Asia to China. Ecologically important in arid ecosystems. Subject to large-scale captive breeding and release programmes in Saudi Arabia and the UAE for falconry.

children.name: Tetrax
children.description: The little bustard — a monotypic genus of small, compact bustards found across southern Europe, North Africa, and Asia. Unlike their larger relatives, little bustards are gregarious, forming large flocks in winter. They are fast runners and explosive fliers.

children.name: Tetrax tetrax
children.description: The smallest European bustard, at just 40-45 cm. Sandy-brown above, white below, with a distinctive black-and-white neck pattern in breeding males. Found in open grassland and farmland across southern Europe, North Africa, and Central Asia. Classified as Near Threatened due to agricultural intensification.
